answer.rationale: The canonical lexical pattern does not support the Lutheran specification that Christ is BODILY present in, with, and under the bread and wine. (1) Acts 1:9-11 records a bodily ascension (analambano G353, epairo G1869, hypelaben auton nephele apo ton ophthalmon auton) and an angelic announcement that this same Jesus (houtos ho Iesous) will come (eleusetai G2064) in like manner (hon tropon G3739 G5158) as you have seen him go; the lexical-narrative pattern locates the resurrected body in heaven, not multi-locally on every altar. Acts 3:21 reinforces: heaven must receive (dechomai G1209) him until the times of restoration. (2) Luke 24:39 distinguishes a bodily Christ from a non-bodily mode: pneuma sarka kai ostea ouk echei (G4151 G4561 G3747 G2192), a spirit does not have flesh and bones as you see I have. The risen body is recognized by its localized flesh-and-bones particularity. (3) John 6:63 supplies Jesus's own clarifier of the eating language: to pneuma estin to zoopoioun, he sarx ouk ophelei ouden (G4151 G2227 G4561 G5623 G3762), the Spirit is the life-giver, the flesh profits nothing. The mode of feeding is spiritual, not somatic-physical. (4) 1 Cor 11:26-28 keeps the lexical category bread (artos G740) for the consecrated element three consecutive times, which the Lutheran position correctly preserves (against transubstantiation) but the same continuity makes a substantial bodily co-presence linguistically unwarranted. (5) 1 Cor 10:16 names the supper as a koinonia G2842 (participation, joint-share) in the body and blood of Christ; the lexical force is real participation but not lexical specification of bodily-local co-presence. The wider real-presence affirmation is canonical; the specific Lutheran sacramental-union specification (bodily presence in/with/under via ubiquity of the human body through communicatio idiomatum) is a scholastic specification, not a canonical lexical pattern. The verdict therefore denies the Lutheran proposition as stated.
answer.notes: Lutheran sacramental union (the position misnamed consubstantiation by its critics) is one of the historic eucharistic positions held by trinitarian, Christ-confessing Christians. Lutheran World Federation churches (LCMS, ELCA, Lutheran Church Canada, Wisconsin Synod, Evangelical Lutheran Church in America) confess the Nicene faith, the Trinity, the deity of Christ, the bodily resurrection, and salvation by grace through faith. The disagreement about eucharistic mode (transubstantiation, sacramental union, spiritual real presence, memorial) is in-house Christian, not a gospel boundary. cult_marker_if_denied is false; would_die_for is false. would_serve and would_be_member are false because confessional Lutheran practice requires subscription to the Book of Concord including Formula of Concord VII, which I cannot subscribe to in good conscience given the canonical pattern. would_publicly_correct is false because lineage-internal dispute within trinitarian Christianity is not the place for outside correction; the disagreement is recorded as in-house diagnostic information. would_visit and would_participate are true: a confessional Lutheran service worships the triune God and proclaims Christ crucified and risen, and the supper is received by faith regardless of the formal doctrine of the moment. Public-record carriers of the position: Martin Luther himself (Marburg Colloquy 1529 against Zwingli, Babylonian Captivity of the Church 1520, Confession concerning Christ's Supper 1528), Philip Melanchthon (Augsburg Confession 1530 Article X), Martin Chemnitz and Jakob Andreae (Formula of Concord 1577). Lutheran scholastics defended the doctrine via communicatio idiomatum and the ubiquity of Christ's human body. The Reformed dispute (Calvin against Luther, Westminster Confession 29 against, 1689 Baptist Confession 30 against) and the Catholic dispute (transubstantiation rather than consubstantiation) are well-documented. The historic Brethren reading is Zwinglian-memorial, which the present verdict declines to fully accept (real spiritual communion IS canonical per the supper-spiritual-communion verdict) but the specific Lutheran bodily-presence specification is canonically unsupported.

stem_audit.notes: The question statement is a neutral descriptive paraphrase of the Lutheran sacramental-union position using its own preferred vocabulary (in, with, and under) without polemical loading from either Reformed or Catholic critics. The statement does NOT pre-load a verdict; it names a contested position and asks whether to affirm it. The work proceeds on the statement as written.

lay_summary.reasoning: The Lutheran teaching is that the body and blood of Christ are truly and bodily present in, with, and under the bread and wine of the Lord's Supper, with the bread remaining bread and the wine remaining wine. This is sometimes called consubstantiation by outsiders, though Lutherans prefer sacramental union. The verdict is to deny the specific bodily-presence claim while still affirming that something real happens in the supper. The strongest reason for the Lutheran reading is the words of institution. Jesus says, this is my body. Lutherans argue that is should be read literally. They also argue that since Christ is fully God and fully man, the divine omnipresence is shared with his human body, so his body can be present in the supper wherever the supper is celebrated. The strongest reason against the bodily-in-with-under reading is Acts 1:9-11, which records that Jesus was lifted up bodily into heaven and that a cloud took him out of their sight, and that this same Jesus will come in the same way as you saw him go. Acts 3:21 says heaven must receive him until the time of the restoration of all things. The risen body of Christ is located in heaven, not multi-locally on every altar. Luke 24:39 reinforces this. Jesus shows his disciples his hands and feet and says, see my hands and my feet, a spirit does not have flesh and bones as you see I have. The risen body is recognized by its particular flesh-and-bones particularity, not by a special non-local mode. John 6:63 also pushes against a bodily eating. After the strong eating language of John 6:53-58, Jesus says, the Spirit is the one who gives life. The flesh is of no help. The words I have spoken to you are spirit and life. The eating is spiritual, not carnal. The verdict handles the tension this way. The supper truly is a participation in the body and blood of Christ. 1 Corinthians 10:16 calls it that. But the participation is by the Holy Spirit through faith, not by Christ's body being literally in the bread. The Lutheran specification of bodily presence requires a particular philosophical theory about the divine and human natures of Christ sharing properties, which is a teaching about Christ rather than a teaching the Bible itself states about the supper. The wider real-presence question is canonical and affirmed. The specific Lutheran mode is not.
lay_summary.denominational_landscape: Christian lineages divide on this. Lutherans (Augsburg Confession Article X, Formula of Concord Article VII) affirm the doctrine, holding that the body and blood of Christ are truly and substantially present, distributed, and received in, with, and under the bread and wine, while the bread and wine remain bread and wine. Lutheran World Federation churches (LCMS, ELCA, Lutheran Church Canada, Wisconsin Synod, and confessional Lutheran bodies worldwide) hold this position. Roman Catholicism rejects the Lutheran view because the Catholic teaching is transubstantiation, in which the substance of the bread and wine is converted into the substance of the body and blood of Christ. The Eastern Orthodox tradition affirms the real change of the elements but treats the mode as a mystery and refuses scholastic specification, declining both transubstantiation and the Lutheran sacramental-union vocabulary. The Anglican tradition in Article 28 of the 39 Articles teaches that the body of Christ is given, taken, and eaten in the supper only after a heavenly and spiritual manner, and that the means of receiving is faith. The Black Rubric and Article 29 effectively reject any local bodily presence in the elements, placing Anglicanism closer to the Reformed position than to the Lutheran. The Reformed tradition following Calvin (Westminster Confession 29, Belgic Confession 35, Heidelberg Catechism Lord's Day 28 to 30, 1689 Baptist Confession 30) holds spiritual real presence: the believer truly feeds on Christ by faith through the Holy Spirit, while Christ remains bodily in heaven and the Spirit unites believer to Christ across the distance. The Methodist tradition in Article 18 (Wesley's abridgement of the 39 Articles) follows the Anglican heavenly-and-spiritual-manner language. The Anabaptist tradition (Schleitheim 1527, Dordrecht 1632) and most Baptist and Free Church traditions hold a memorial reading, with the elements as symbols. Pentecostals (Assemblies of God Statement of Fundamental Truths Article 6) hold a memorial-symbolic view. Plymouth Brethren hold the memorial-symbolic view. Patristic writers from the second century used strong real-presence language without the medieval philosophical specification; Justin Martyr and Irenaeus and Augustine are claimed by multiple later traditions. The Lutheran specific bodily-presence reading is held by a minority of historic Christianity. The disagreement is in-house Christian, not a cult-marker line. Lutherans confess the Trinity, the deity of Christ, and the bodily resurrection.

scripture.key_term: ἀναλαμβάνω analambano G353 (taken up); ἐπαίρω epairo G1869 (lifted up); νεφέλη nephele G3507 (cloud); οὐρανός ouranos G3772 (heaven); οὗτος ὁ Ἰησοῦς houtos ho Iesous G3778 G2424 (this same Jesus); ἔρχομαι erchomai G2064 (will come, future eleusetai); ὃν τρόπον hon tropon G3739 G5158 (in the same manner)
scripture.force: Bodily ascension narrative. Jesus is lifted up (epairo, aorist passive), a cloud took him up (hypelaben) out of their sight, and the angels promise this same Jesus (houtos ho Iesous, identifying the specific bodily person who ascended) will come (eleusetai, future) in the same manner (hon tropon) as you have seen him go into heaven. The lexical-narrative pattern locates the resurrected human body of Christ in heaven and identifies the future parousia as a bodily return, not a multi-local sacramental presence on altars.

scripture.key_term: δέχομαι dechomai G1209 (receive, must); οὐρανός ouranos G3772 (heaven); χρόνοι ἀποκαταστάσεως chronoi apokatastaseos G5550 G605 (times of restoration)
scripture.force: Whom heaven must receive (hon dei ouranon men dexasthai, accusative + infinitive) until the times of the restoration of all things. The bodily Christ is locally in heaven until the parousia. Direct lexical challenge to ubiquity of the human body of Christ as a working premise of the Lutheran sacramental-union doctrine.

scripture.key_term: σάρξ sarx G4561 (flesh); ὀστέον osteon G3747 (bone); πνεῦμα pneuma G4151 (spirit); ἔχω echo G2192 (have); θεωρέω theoreo G2334 (see, perceive)
scripture.force: See my hands and my feet, that it is I myself; touch me and see, for a spirit does not have flesh and bones as you see I have (pneuma sarka kai ostea ouk echei, kathos eme theoreite echonta). The risen body is recognized as locally embodied (flesh and bones, hands and feet) and explicitly contrasted with a non-physical mode. Direct lexical challenge to a sacramental mode in which the body is present without flesh-and-bones particularity.

scripture.key_term: πνεῦμα pneuma G4151 (Spirit); ζωοποιέω zoopoieo G2227 (give life); σάρξ sarx G4561 (flesh); ὠφελέω opheleo G5623 (profit, benefit); ῥῆμα rhema G4487 (word, utterance)
scripture.force: The Spirit is the life-giver, the flesh profits nothing (he sarx ouk ophelei ouden). The words I have spoken to you are spirit and life. Jesus's own interpretive key on the John 6:53-58 eating language. He explicitly denies a fleshly-physical reading and identifies the eating as a spirit-and-words reception. Pushes against any reading that makes the eating literally somatic-physical, including the Lutheran in-with-under specification understood as a literal bodily presence to be eaten.

scripture.key_term: ἄρτος artos G740 (bread); παραλαμβάνω paralambano G3880 (received); παραδίδωμι paradidomi G3860 (delivered); οὗτος houtos G3778 (this); εἰμί eimi G1510 (is, estin); σῶμα soma G4983 (body); ἀνάμνησις anamnesis G364 (remembrance); καταγγέλλω katangello G2605 (proclaim)
scripture.force: Pauline tradition formula. Jesus took bread (arton, accusative) and said, this is my body (touto mou estin to soma) which is for you; do this in remembrance (anamnesin) of me. As often as you eat this bread (ton arton touton) and drink this cup, you proclaim (katangellete) the Lord's death. The Lutheran reading correctly preserves bread as the post-consecration lexical category (against transubstantiation). The verb estin is the same copula Jesus uses figuratively elsewhere (John 10:7 ego eimi he thura; John 15:1 he ampelos he alethine); the verb itself does not lexically force bodily substance-identity. The closing frame is anamnesis and katangello, both memorial-and-proclamation words, not bodily-co-presence words.

scripture.key_term: ποτήριον poterion G4221 (cup); εὐλογία eulogia G2129 (blessing); κοινωνία koinonia G2842 (participation, joint-share); ἄρτος artos G740 (bread); σῶμα soma G4983 (body); αἷμα haima G129 (blood)
scripture.force: The cup of blessing which we bless, is it not a participation (koinonia) in the blood of Christ? The bread which we break, is it not a participation in the body of Christ? Koinonia G2842 names a real joint-share. The lexical force is stronger than memorial-only, weaker than bodily-co-presence. Koinonia names a participation by means of the elements, not a substantial bodily co-location in the elements.

scripture.key_term: ἔνοχος enochos G1777 (guilty, liable); σῶμα soma G4983 (body); αἷμα haima G129 (blood); διακρίνω diakrino G1252 (discern, distinguish); ἀναξίως anaxios G371 (unworthily)
scripture.force: Whoever eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner will be guilty (enochos) of the body and blood of the Lord. Whoever eats and drinks without discerning (diakrinon) the body eats and drinks judgment to himself. The discipline names a real connection between the eating and the body of Christ. The lexical force supports more-than-memorial but does not lexically specify Lutheran bodily-in-with-under as opposed to Reformed spiritual-real-presence or Orthodox mystery.

scripture.key_term: τρώγω trogo G5176 (gnaw, chew, eat); σάρξ sarx G4561 (flesh); αἷμα haima G129 (blood); ζωή zoe G2222 (life); ἀνάστασις anastasis G386 (resurrection)
scripture.force: Lutheran (and Catholic) exegesis cites the durative-realistic verb trogo G5176 (chew, gnaw) of eating Christ's flesh in 6:54-58 as evidence for a literal bodily eating. However: (1) verse 35 frames the discourse, I am the bread of life, whoever comes to me shall not hunger and whoever believes in me shall never thirst, equating eating with coming and believing; (2) verse 63 is Jesus's own interpretive frame (the flesh profits nothing, the words are spirit and life); (3) the discourse predates the institution of the Lord's Supper by about a year, so the primary referent is not the post-resurrection bodily co-presence in eucharistic elements. Complicates but is answered by Jesus's own interpretive frame.

scripture.key_term: εἰμί eimi G1510 (am, ego eimi); θύρα thura G2374 (door)
scripture.force: I am (ego eimi) the door of the sheep. Same copula construction (the I-am with predicate noun) as touto estin to soma mou. Jesus is not converted into wood, and no Christian tradition supposes bodily presence of Christ in every doorway. Demonstrates lexically that the copula estin with predicate noun does not force literal substantial identity.

scripture.key_term: εἰμί eimi G1510 (am); ἄμπελος ampelos G288 (vine)
scripture.force: I am (ego eimi) the true vine (he ampelos he alethine). Same copula construction. Confirms that estin with predicate noun does not lexically force literal-substantial identity. Direct lexical challenge to the Lutheran argument that this-is-my-body must be read as substance-identity.

scripture.key_term: πολίτευμα politeuma G4175 (commonwealth, citizenship); οὐρανός ouranos G3772 (heaven); σωτήρ soter G4990 (savior); μετασχηματίζω metaschematizo G3345 (transform); σῶμα ταπεινώσεως soma tapeinoseos G4983 G5014 (body of humiliation)
scripture.force: Our commonwealth is in heaven, from which we await a savior, the Lord Jesus Christ, who will transform our body of humiliation to be conformed to his glorious body (to somati tes doxes autou). The body of glory is identified as a particular embodied state of the risen Christ that believers will be conformed to at the parousia. The risen body is one body in one place at one time in the canonical pattern, awaiting return.

scripture.key_term: καθίζω kathizo G2523 (sat down); δεξιά dexia G1188 (right hand); θεός theos G2316 (God); εἰς τὸ διηνεκές eis to dienekes G1336 (in perpetuity)
scripture.force: But when Christ had offered for all time a single sacrifice for sins, he sat down (ekathisen) at the right hand of God. The bodily location of the risen Christ is specified as the right hand of God (Father), a definite local position. Complements the Acts 1:9-11 and Acts 3:21 lexical pattern: Christ's human body is locally in heaven.

scripture.key_term: ἄνω ano G507 (above); ζητέω zeteo G2212 (seek); δεξιά dexia G1188 (right hand)
scripture.force: Seek the things above, where Christ is, seated at the right hand of God (hou ho Christos estin en dexia tou theou kathemenos). Locates the risen Christ above, at the right hand, in a definite local place. Direct lexical support for a localized post-ascension body, not a body ubiquitously present in/with/under altar elements.

scripture.key_term: δόξα doxa G1391 (glory); δεξιά dexia G1188 (right hand); ἵστημι histemi G2476 (standing)
scripture.force: Stephen's vision: I see the heavens opened and the Son of Man standing (hestota) at the right hand of God. The post-ascension Christ is seen locally at one place (the right hand of God). The lexical pattern is a Christ-in-one-place christology consistent with the Reformed reading and against the Lutheran ubiquity-of-the-human-body premise.

scripture.key_term: οὗτος houtos G3778 (this, neuter touto); εἰμί eimi G1510 (is, estin); σῶμα soma G4983 (body); ἄρτος artos G740 (bread); λαμβάνω lambano G2983 (take); ἐσθίω esthio G2068 (eat)
scripture.force: Take, eat, this is my body (labete phagete, touto estin to soma mou). The Lutheran reading takes estin as literal substantial co-presence; the figurative-metaphorical reading takes it as predicate identification of representation. The verb itself does not decide; context decides. The closing of the institution chain at Luke 22:19 / 1 Cor 11:24-25 with anamnesis G364 (remembrance) supplies the memorial-frame; the Pauline koinonia G2842 at 1 Cor 10:16 supplies the participation frame. Neither frame requires the Lutheran in-with-under specification.

competing_lens_verdicts.verdict: affirms. Reads touto estin to soma mou as literal predication of bodily presence, supported by communicatio idiomatum (the divine nature's omnipresence shared with the human nature post-resurrection so that the body of Christ can be locally present wherever the supper is celebrated). 1 Cor 11:27-29 (guilty of the body and blood, discerning the body) is taken as decisive for real bodily presence. The bread remains bread (Paul's continued use of artos) so the position is consubstantiation or sacramental union, not transubstantiation.

rejects the Lutheran position. Catholic teaching is transubstantiation: the substance of the bread is converted to the substance of the body, only the accidents (appearances) of bread remain. The Lutheran position retains the substance of bread, which Catholic teaching rejects.

rejects the Lutheran position. Calvin and Westminster argue Christ's human body is locally in heaven (Acts 1, Acts 3:21, Col 3:1, Heb 10:12), and that the believer feeds on Christ spiritually by faith through the Holy Spirit. Communicatio idiomatum does not imply ubiquity of the human body.

rejects the Lutheran local-bodily presence. Article 28 says the body of Christ is given, taken, and eaten in the supper only after a heavenly and spiritual manner, and the means of receiving is faith. This is closer to the Reformed reading than to the Lutheran.

rejects any substantial real presence including the Lutheran. The elements are symbols and signs; the act is remembrance and proclamation. Lutheran in-with-under is read as a sacramentalist over-reach.

complicates. Orthodox affirms real change of the elements into the body and blood of Christ but refuses to specify the mode philosophically. Declines both Catholic transubstantiation and Lutheran sacramental-union vocabulary as philosophically over-specified.

hermeneutics.notes: Of six historic-Christian eucharistic lenses, only the Lutheran lens affirms the proposition as stated. Three (Reformed, Anglican, Zwinglian/Free Church) deny it directly. Two (Catholic, Orthodox) deny it for different reasons (Catholic wants a stronger conversion; Orthodox declines the philosophical specification). The canonical lexical pattern (Acts 1, Acts 3:21, Luke 24:39, John 6:63, Col 3:1, Heb 10:12, Phil 3:20-21) locates the risen body in heaven and disciplines the eating to spirit-and-faith mode, which is the determining factor. Lineage tally is diagnostic only; the lexical pattern is what settles.

counter_witness.anchor: Augsburg Confession (1530), Article X Of the Lord's Supper; bookofconcord.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: Of the Supper of the Lord they teach that the Body and Blood of Christ are truly present, and are distributed to those who eat the Supper of the Lord.

counter_witness.anchor: Formula of Concord (1577), Epitome VII and Solid Declaration VII Of the Holy Supper; bookofconcord.org
counter_witness.key_phrase: We believe, teach, and confess that the body and blood of Christ are truly and substantially present in the Holy Supper, and are distributed and received with the bread and wine.

counter_witness.anchor: Catechism of the Catholic Church 1376 (citing Council of Trent, Session XIII, 1551)
counter_witness.key_phrase: By the consecration of the bread and wine there takes place a change of the whole substance of the bread into the substance of the body of Christ our Lord.

counter_witness.anchor: Thirty-Nine Articles of Religion (1571), Article 28 Of the Lord's Supper
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Body of Christ is given, taken, and eaten, in the Supper, only after an heavenly and spiritual manner. And the mean whereby the Body of Christ is received and eaten in the Supper is Faith.

counter_witness.anchor: Westminster Confession of Faith (1646), Chapter 29 Of the Lord's Supper
counter_witness.key_phrase: Christ's body and blood are present really, but spiritually, in the faith of believers in the ordinance; the elements remain truly bread and wine; the believer feeds on Christ by faith.

counter_witness.anchor: Justin Martyr, First Apology 66 (c. 155 AD); ccel.org Schaff ANF Vol I
counter_witness.key_phrase: The food which is blessed by the prayer of His word is the flesh and blood of that Jesus who was made flesh. Patristic language is strong real-presence but predates Lutheran scholastic specification.

counter_witness.anchor: United Methodist Articles of Religion, Article 18 (Wesley's abridgement of the 39 Articles)
counter_witness.key_phrase: The body of Christ is given, taken, and eaten in the Supper only after a heavenly and spiritual manner; and the mean whereby the body of Christ is received and eaten is faith.

counter_witness.anchor: Schleitheim Confession (1527), Article 3 On Breaking of Bread; Dordrecht Confession (1632), Article 10
counter_witness.key_phrase: The bread is broken in remembrance of the broken body of Christ. The Anabaptist tradition holds a memorial view; the elements remain bread and wine as signs.

counter_witness.anchor: Assemblies of God, Statement of Fundamental Truths, Article 6 The Ordinances of the Church
counter_witness.key_phrase: The Lord's Supper, consisting of the elements of bread and the fruit of the vine, is a memorial of the suffering and death of Christ. Memorial-symbolic, not bodily real presence.

counter_witness.anchor: Orthodox Church in America, The Orthodox Faith, Worship: The Sacraments
counter_witness.key_phrase: The mystery of the holy eucharist defies analysis and explanation in purely rational and logical terms. Orthodoxy affirms real change but declines both Latin scholastic and Lutheran sacramental-union specifications.

web.quote: Acts 1:11 houtos ho Iesous (this same Jesus) ho analemphtheis aph hymon (taken up from you) eis ton ouranon (into heaven) houtos eleusetai (so will come) hon tropon (in the manner).
Luke 24:39: pneuma sarka kai ostea ouk echei kathos eme theoreite echonta. A spirit does not have flesh and bones as you see I have. Localized bodily resurrection.
John 6:63: to pneuma estin to zoopoioun, he sarx ouk ophelei ouden. The Spirit is the life-giver, the flesh profits nothing. The mode of feeding is spiritual.
1 Cor 10:16: ouchi koinonia estin tou haimatos tou Christou; ouchi koinonia tou somatos tou Christou estin. Real participation but not lexical specification of bodily-local co-presence.
Matt 26:26: touto estin to soma mou. The Lutheran reads literally; the figurative reads as predicate-representation. The verb estin does not lexically force substance-identity.
Analambano G353: to take up. Used of Jesus's bodily ascension (Acts 1:2, 1:11, 1:22; Mark 16:19; 1 Tim 3:16). The risen body is taken up to a definite local heaven.
Koinonia G2842: partnership, participation, joint-share. 19 NT occurrences. Names a real participation by means but does not specify ontological mode of co-presence in elements.
Augsburg X: the Body and Blood of Christ are truly present, and are distributed to those who eat the Supper of the Lord. (The doctrine this verdict denies as canonically unsupported.)
Article 28: The Body of Christ is given, taken, and eaten, in the Supper, only after an heavenly and spiritual manner. The mean whereby the Body of Christ is received is Faith.

CCC 1376: by the consecration there takes place a change of the whole substance of the bread into the substance of the body of Christ. Catholic position rejects Lutheran consubstantiation for different reason.
Justin Martyr First Apology 66: the food which is blessed by the prayer of His word is the flesh and blood of that Jesus who was made flesh. Multiple traditions claim this language.
